Let's dive more deeply into the benefits of being an authority rather than merely in authority. Now the key motivator from being seen as an authority is the recognition that typically we're all better off following the guidance of experts. So if we can show others that we have true expertise beyond their own, we've given them an invaluable reason to follow our recommendations. Fortunately, we don't always have to be an expert on an issue to use the power of authority to influence others in our direction. Sometimes all we have to do is to point to the opinion of a legitimate authority who has taken a position consistent with or favoring our recommendation. Enlisting the influence of such an expert has been shown to be a powerful lever for change, and as long as we do so honestly, it's an ethical practice as well. Plus, there is something we can do to enhance the impact of a favorable expert's advice even further. Multiply it. Audiences trust and follow the counsel of a set of experts more than any one of them. Thus, if you do the work of collecting and then spotlighting support from multiple experts, you will be more successful than a rival who settles for claiming the support of just one.
